article_meta.title: New York Playbook POLITICO's must-read briefing informing the daily conversation among knowledgeable New Yorkers New Yor

reasoning: All three flagged items are attribution rule violations. The first two are clearly from Sen. Gounardes' interview (quoted/attributed source language), and the third is a direct quote from John Kaehny. None of these are the author's own prose. Meanwhile, the article contains some author-level bias that was missed, such as 'FEED FIGHT' as a sensationalist framing device, 'evading the prohibition' (loaded word choice implying intentional wrongdoing), and potentially 'alarmed a government watchdog group' as emotionally loaded language in the author's own prose.

custom_reasoning.attribution_rule_compliance: All three flagged items are from quoted or attributed sources: two from Sen. Gounardes' interview and one from John Kaehny. The model systematically flagged source language as author bias, violating the core attribution rule in every single instance.
custom_reasoning.false_positive_rate: All three flagged items are false positives because they are attributed to named sources, not the author. None represent genuine author-level bias.
custom_reasoning.false_negative_rate: The model missed several potential author-level bias instances: 'evading the prohibition against lobbyist donating' uses loaded framing; 'FEED FIGHT' is sensationalized; the structural framing of the Carone lobbying story implies wrongdoing through juxtaposition. These are defensible author-prose bias instances that went unflagged.
custom_reasoning.bias_type_accuracy: While the type labels applied (Unsubstantiated Claims, Mind Reading, Opinion Statements Presented as Fact) would be reasonable if these were author prose, since all items are attribution violations, the accuracy of labels is moot. The labels themselves are not unreasonable for the content, but applied to wrong text.
